exemplar.title:
What is 
the effect of time, measured at 15, 30, 45, and 60 minutes, on the diffusion of paracetamol 
through a partially permeable membrane, measured by titration against 0.05M of sodium 
hydroxide?

detailedExemplarData.title:
What is 
the effect of time, measured at 15, 30, 45, and 60 minutes, on the diffusion of paracetamol 
through a partially permeable membrane, measured by titration against 0.05M of sodium 
hydroxide?

strengths: Data tables and bar graph allow the reader to follow processed results
Uncertainties from volumetric glassware are propagated into mass values
Correct stoichiometric equation for titration is presented
weaknesses: Cluttered headings, missing or misplaced units in tables and graph
60 min data set is absent and not explained
Significant omissions in uncertainty consideration (e.g., titrant concentration) and inconsistent rounding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data is either clear or precise but not both, and significant formatting issues and missing time-point data hinder full clarity (band 1-2). Consideration of uncertainties shows major omissions (band 1-2), and data processing contains inaccuracies and gaps (band 1-2). The accumulation of these weaknesses supports a score of 2 for Data Analysis.

strengths: Research question situated within a specific pharmaceutical context
Detailed apparatus list with uncertainties for glassware
weaknesses: Background section is citation-heavy and somewhat cluttered
Sample size and choice of time points lack justification
Procedure contains minor ambiguities (e.g., crushing method, âxâ minutes placeholder)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the research question is clearly positioned in an appropriate context (band 5-6) and the controlled-variable table demonstrates strong methodological awareness, other strands reveal only a described rather than fully explained rationale for data collection (band 3-4) and minor ambiguities in the procedure that limit complete reproducibility (band 3-4). Overall this justifies a mid-band score of 3 for Research Design.

strengths: Conclusion restates the observed trend and relates it to diffusion theory
Comparison to scientific context (Fickâs law, ionisation) aligns findings with theory
weaknesses: Quantitative support is limited and uncertainties are not integrated into the conclusion
Omission of the 60 min data point reduces completeness of trend analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is described and shows consistency with analysis but lacks full justification through integrated uncertainties and complete data (band 1-2). The comparison to scientific context is relevant but remains superficial without depth on quantitative agreement (band 1-2). These shortcomings warrant a score of 2 for Conclusion.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are clearly described (membrane model, pH/temperature, knot variability) with reasoned impact on results
Realistic, relevant improvements are proposed and explained for each weakness (e.g., water bath, pH buffer, colorimeter)
weaknesses: Relative prioritisation of suggested improvements is not discussed
Some weaknesses (e.g., knot-tying variability) lack quantitative assessment of impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation effectively describes specific methodological weaknesses and explains how each could influence results (band 3-4), and for every weakness it proposes realistic, well-explained improvements (band 5-6). On balance, this performance sits at the top of the 3-4 band, meriting a score of 4 for Evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question (effect of diffusion time on the amount of acetaminophen diffusing through a visking tube) is clearly stated, medically motivated and placed within a specific pharmaceutical/drug-delivery context. Background paragraphs link migraines, drug absorption, diffusion theory and titration, giving a reader an explicit rationale for why the question matters. This matches the descriptor of âdescribed within a specific and appropriate context,â warranting the 5-6 band; minor digressions and some citation clutter justify awarding the lower end of that band (5).

Calculations of moles and masses follow correct stoichiometry and are summarized in tables and a graph, demonstrating appropriate processing. Nevertheless, rounding errors, an unexplained absence of the 60-min set, and an incorrect mean volume (0.967 mL) indicate notable inaccuracies. Hence the strand sits in band 3-4, with 3 marks awarded.

Data tables and a bar graph are provided, but headings are cluttered, units sometimes missing or misplaced, and the 60-min data set is absent. Despite these issues, the reader can still follow what was measured and how it was processed, so communication is clear OR precise but not both, matching the 3-4 band; the lower mark (3) is applied owing to formatting problems.

For every stated weakness an explicit, realistic improvement is proposed (water bath and pH buffer, rubber bands of equal length, colourimeter). The explanations link each suggestion to how it would mitigate the error and improve data quality, satisfying the criteria for âexplains relevant realistic improvements.â Minor lack of prioritisation keeps the score to 5 within the 5-6 band.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted and a propagation is attempted for moles and mass. However, only mean values are treated, NaOH concentration uncertainty is ignored, and the calculation method (0.04/0.967) is conceptually weak. These significant omissions place the work in the middle band (3-4) but at the lower score of 3.

The student compares findings to Fickâs law, pH-dependent ionisation and membrane permeability, engaging with relevant scientific literature. The discussion is appropriate but somewhat superficial and lacks depth on quantitative comparison, placing it comfortably in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Three concrete methodological weaknesses (membrane model, pH/temperature, knot variability) are described with explanation of how each could lower diffusion or add variability, showing understanding of their impact. The impact is not prioritised or quantitatively assessed, so the work aligns with âdescribes specific weaknessesâ at the top of the 3-4 band.

The student identifies independent, dependent and numerous controlled variables, explains why each control matters, and plans three repeats at four time points (although the 60-min data are later absent). This shows a clear intention to collect relevant and sufficient data. However, there is no justification of sample size, no discussion of why only four time points were chosen, and limited attention to controlling NaOH concentration drift. Hence the considerations are âdescribedâ rather than âfully explained,â fitting the upper boundary of the 3-4 band.

The apparatus list includes quantities and uncertainties; the step-by-step procedure supplies volumes, masses and times sufficient for another student to replicate the work with only minor ambiguities (e.g., exact crushing method, knot-tying style, omission of 60-min step). Because reproduction is possible but not completely unambiguous, the work aligns with âallows reproduction with few ambiguities,â meriting the upper mark in the 3-4 band.

The conclusion restates the observed trend and connects it to diffusion concepts, showing consistency with the processed data. Quantitative support is limited and uncertainties are not integrated, so the justification is partial rather than full. This meets the descriptor for 3-4 marks; 3 marks are awarded.

annotations.comment: The background section is citation-heavy and somewhat cluttered. Consider integrating key references more selectively to improve clarity and flow.
The research question is articulated within a clear pharmaceutical context, linking migraines, drug absorption, and diffusion theory to justify the investigationâs relevance.
The choice of four diffusion time points (15, 30, 45, 60 min) is stated without justification. Explain why these intervals were selected to strengthen your methodological rationale.
There is no justification of sample size or the number of repeats. Discuss how three replicates were determined to ensure sufficient statistical power.
The controlled-variable table comprehensively lists variables, controls, and rationales. This clarity aids reproducibility and highlights methodological rigor.
Phenolphthalein volume is controlled as â5 drops,â which is imprecise. Specify a volume (e.g., 0.25 mL) and include instrument uncertainty to improve accuracy.
The apparatus list includes uncertainties for volumetric glassware, demonstrating attention to measurement precision and facilitating uncertainty propagation.
The method for crushing tablets is vague. Describe mortar-and-pestle technique (grit size, duration) to ensure precise replication.
Step 8 uses âxâ minutes rather than specifying each diffusion time. Replace âxâ with the actual time intervals to remove ambiguity.
Only three test tubes are filled but four time points are planned. Include a fourth tube for the 60 min trial to match your experimental design.
The table header contains a misprint (âTADIC J. TIU AUUII INESUITSâ). Correct the title to clearly label titration results.
The 60 min data set is absent from the quantitative findings. Include it or explain its omission to ensure completeness of the data record.
The data table combines clear layout with accurate propagation of burette uncertainties, supporting transparent processing.
The titration equation $$C_8H_9NO_2 + NaOH \rightarrow C_8H_8NNaO_2 + H_2O$$ is correctly presented, demonstrating proper stoichiometric understanding.
The uncertainty discussion omits the titrant concentration uncertainty. Incorporate the Â±0.05 M NaOH uncertainty to fully account for systematic errors.
The table of moles for each trial is clear but lacks the 60 min entry. Ensure all time points are processed and reported for consistency.
Rounding of mass values in Table 5 is inconsistent (e.g., 12.8 vs 12.10 mg). Apply uniform significant-figure rules across the dataset.
Graph 1âs axes lack detailed tick labels and units. Enhance axis annotation to improve clarity and precise interpretation.
The interpretation links trends to passive diffusion and concentration gradients, effectively connecting data to theory.
The interpretation stops at 45 min and omits discussion of the 60 min point. Include all data in your trend analysis for completeness.
The formula for uncertainty propagation (Uncertainty = moles Ã relative volume uncertainty) is conceptually incomplete. Include all contributing uncertainties.
The numerical application in $$0.0000483\times(0.04/0.967)$$ is correct but lacks conceptual clarity on why concentration uncertainty is omitted.
The uncertainty in mass table neatly translates aqueous uncertainties into mg, making the error analysis transparent.
The conclusion accurately restates the main trend but does not integrate uncertainty bounds. Consider adding uncertainty to demonstrate robustness.
The evaluation identifies specific systematic errors (membrane model, pH, temperature), demonstrating a clear understanding of methodological impact.
Random errors in knot-tying are described but not prioritized by their relative impact. Consider ranking errors to focus improvements.
Using a water bath and pH buffer to maintain physiological conditions is a well-targeted improvement that directly addresses systematic errors.
Proposing a colorimeter to detect end-point pink colour enhances precision by reducing observer bias in titration end detection.
